content_markdown:
A mass at point $X$ gives rise to a gravitational field strength $g$ at point $P$ as shown below.

An identical mass is placed at point $Y$ as shown below.

The resultant gravitational field strength at $P$ is now

diagram_metadata.description: Two diagrams showing point masses and a point P. In the first, mass X is to the left of P. In the second, mass X is to the left of P and an identical mass Y is to the right of P, further away from P than X is.

vibe_explanation: Gravitational field strength is a vector quantity directed towards the mass. Initially, the field at $P$ is $g$ directed towards $X$ (to the left). When the second mass is placed at $Y$, it creates a field at $P$ directed towards $Y$ (to the right). Since $Y$ is further from $P$ than $X$ is, its field strength $g_Y$ is less than $g$. The resultant field is the vector sum: $g - g_Y$. Since $0 < g_Y < g$, the magnitude of the resultant field must be between $g$ and zero.
